cards.text: What are the stages of spermatogenesis?
cards.answer: Spermatogonia (2n) -> primary spermatocytes (2n) -> meiosis I -> secondary spermatocytes (n) -> meiosis II -> spermatids (n) -> spermiogenesis (maturation) -> spermatozoa. Takes ~74 days. Temperature-sensitive: testes 2-3 degrees below body temperature. FSH + testosterone required.

tip.tip: From stem cell to mature sperm in 74 days.

cards.text: Describe the hormonal changes during the menstrual cycle.
cards.answer: Follicular phase: FSH stimulates follicle growth, rising oestrogen. Mid-cycle: positive feedback of oestrogen triggers LH surge -> ovulation. Luteal phase: corpus luteum secretes progesterone + oestrogen. If no pregnancy, corpus luteum degenerates -> hormone withdrawal -> menstruation.

tip.tip: Oestrogen dominates first half, progesterone second half.

cards.text: What is the role of hCG in pregnancy?
cards.answer: Human chorionic gonadotropin, secreted by trophoblast/placenta. Maintains corpus luteum (and its progesterone production) during first trimester until placenta takes over steroidogenesis. Basis of pregnancy tests. Doubles every 48 hours in early pregnancy.

tip.tip: Rescues the corpus luteum from degeneration.

cards.text: What are the disorders of sex development?
cards.answer: Klinefelter (47,XXY): tall, small testes, gynaecomastia, infertile. Turner (45,X): short, webbed neck, streak gonads, primary amenorrhoea, coarctation. Complete androgen insensitivity (46,XY): female phenotype, testes present, no uterus. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia: virilisation of females.

cards.text: What hormonal changes occur during puberty?
cards.answer: GnRH pulsatility increases -> FSH and LH rise -> gonadal activation. Males: testosterone -> testicular enlargement (first sign), muscle growth, voice deepening, facial hair. Females: oestrogen -> breast development (thelarche, first sign), menarche. Adrenarche: adrenal androgens -> axillary/pubic hair.

tip.tip: Testicular enlargement and thelarche are the first signs.

cards.answer: Prolactin (anterior pituitary): stimulates milk production (suppressed during pregnancy by oestrogen/progesterone). Oxytocin (posterior pituitary): milk ejection reflex (let-down) triggered by suckling. Suckling maintains prolactin levels and suppresses GnRH (lactational amenorrhoea).

tip.tip: Prolactin makes it, oxytocin ejects it.

cards.text: What is the physiological basis of contraception?
cards.answer: Combined pill: suppresses LH surge (no ovulation) + thickens cervical mucus. Progesterone-only: cervical mucus + endometrial changes. IUD: local inflammatory reaction prevents implantation. Barrier methods: physical block. Emergency contraception: delays ovulation.

Sperm undergoes capacitation in female tract -> acrosome reaction releases enzymes to penetrate zona pellucida -> sperm-oocyte membrane fusion -> cortical reaction (prevents polyspermy) -> completion of meiosis II -> male and female pronuclei fuse -> zygote (46 chromosomes). Usually occurs in ampulla of fallopian tube.

tip.tip: Occurs in the ampulla of the fallopian tube.
